result: Morning light spills across my desk, catching the edge of a chipped mug and the ink stains on my fingers. I like this quiet hour because it feels like a hinge between dreams and the noise of the day. Ideas arrive slowly, like shy cats, then suddenly stretch and demand attention. Sometimes I write about travels I never took, deserts where the wind edits footprints, or cities built on reeds. Other times I jot small observations: the way my neighbor hums while watering plants, the strange confidence of pigeons, the faint metallic smell before rain. These details feel trivial, yet they stitch my days together and keep me from drifting. When the afternoon arrives, my focus frays, so I step outside and watch the sky rearrange itself. Clouds scroll like pages, and I imagine reading them, though their language keeps changing. By evening, the mug is empty, the ink is dry, and the ordinary has quietly become a story worth keeping. I think that is the secret of any good day: noticing enough to remember it later. The world is full of overlooked music, from the refrigerator's steady drone to the distant clack of a train crossing. If I listen closely, those sounds become a kind of map, pointing toward places I might explore in words. So I end the day grateful, not for grand achievements, but for the simple privilege of attention. Tomorrow will offer new scraps to gather, and I will be here, ready to begin all over.
